steps.title: Identify the two-sentence structure
steps.content:
The passage has two sentences. The second begins with “Used to record length and volume, respectively,” a participial phrase that is already grammatically complete. The blank controls where the first sentence ends, so the choice must create a clean sentence boundary before that participial opener.
  • "measurement and used": folds “used to record length and volume, respectively” into the first sentence alongside the coordinating conjunction “and,” creating a redundant, run-on construction. ✗
  • "measurement. Used": ends the first sentence with a period after “measurement,” letting the second sentence stand alone with its participial opener — clean and correct. ✓
  • "measurement, used": uses a comma where a period is needed; the result is a comma splice joining two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. ✗
  • "measurement used": omits all punctuation, fusing the two clauses into a run-on sentence. ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: A period after “measurement” is the only option that correctly separates the two sentences without creating a comma splice or run-on. That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
